section.title: My God, Why Have You Forsaken Me
section.sectionRationale: The crucifixion psalm, its opening cry quoted word for word by Jesus from the cross, Matthew 27:46, and its middle verses describing mocking, thirst, pierced hands and feet, and divided garments with startling precision centuries before crucifixion existed as a punishment. It moves from the deepest possible cry of abandonment to a sweeping vision of worldwide praise, a movement from dereliction to global worship that mirrors the movement from Good Friday to the great commission.
section.thesis: David's cry of abandonment and his enemies' vivid, specific mockery and violence describe, with uncanny precision, the crucifixion of Jesus Christ, whose suffering in forsakenness gives way, just as this psalm does, to a worldwide declaration of God's praise to a people yet unborn.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the opening cry of abandonment against the backdrop of the fathers' trust; the vivid description of mockery, physical agony, and surrounding enemies; the specific details of pierced hands and feet and divided garments; and the closing turn to worldwide praise and proclamation to generations yet unborn.

verses.text: My God, my God, why have You {{azab|forsaken}} me? Why are You so far from saving me, so far from my words of groaning?
I cry out by day, O my God, but You do not answer, and by night, but I have no rest.
Yet You are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.
In You our fathers trusted; they trusted and You delivered them.
They cried out to You and were set free; they trusted in You and were not disappointed.
But I am a worm and not a man, scorned by men and despised by the people.
All who see me mock me; they sneer and shake their heads:
“He trusts in the LORD, let the LORD deliver him; let the LORD rescue him, since He delights in him.”
Yet You brought me forth from the womb; You made me secure at my mother's breast.
From birth I was cast upon You; from my mother's womb You have been my God.

units.label: My strength is dried up like a potsherd

verses.text:
Be not far from me, for trouble is near 
and there is no one to help.
Many bulls surround me; strong bulls of Bashan encircle me.
They open their jaws against me like lions that roar and maul.
I am poured out like water, and all my bones are disjointed. My heart is like wax; it melts away within me.

My strength is dried up like a potsherd, and my tongue sticks to the roof of my mouth. 
You lay me in the dust of death.

units.label: They have pierced my hands and feet

verses.text:
For dogs surround me; a band of evil men encircles me; 
they have {{karah|pierced}} my hands and feet.
I can count all my bones; they stare and gloat over me.
They divide my garments among them and cast {{goral|lots}} for my clothing.
But You, O LORD, be not far off; O my Strength, come quickly to help me.
Deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of wild dogs.
Save me from the mouth of the lion; at the horns of the wild oxen You have answered me!

units.label: All the ends of the earth will remember

verses.text: I will proclaim Your name to my brothers; I will praise You in the assembly.

You who fear the LORD, praise Him! All descendants of Jacob, honor Him! 
All offspring of Israel, revere Him!
For He has not despised or detested the torment of the afflicted. He has not hidden His face from him, but has attended to his cry for help.
My praise for You resounds in the great assembly; I will fulfill my vows before those who fear You.

The poor will eat and be satisfied; those who seek the LORD will praise Him. 
May your hearts live forever!
All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD. All the families of the nations will bow down before Him.
For dominion belongs to the LORD and He rules over the nations.
All the rich of the earth will feast and worship; all who go down to the dust will kneel before Him, even those unable to preserve their lives.
Posterity will serve Him; they will declare the Lord to a new generation.

They will come and proclaim His righteousness to a people yet unborn, 
all that He has done.

azab.gloss: To forsake, abandon, leave. This is the exact word Jesus cries out from the cross in Aramaic, Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani, Matthew 27:46, quoting this psalm's opening cry as He bears the weight of sin in our place.

karah.gloss: Pierced, a difficult but well attested reading of the Hebrew text, rendered by ancient translations including the Septuagint as 'pierced' rather than the alternative reading 'like a lion,' a difference of a single letter in the Hebrew, and confirmed by its startling fulfillment in the nail pierced hands and feet of the crucified Christ.

goral.gloss: Lot, a marked object cast to make a decision or division, here used of soldiers dividing a condemned man's clothing, fulfilled precisely at the cross when the Roman soldiers cast lots for Jesus's garment, John 19:23-24.

basic.context: David begins with the rawest possible cry, feeling utterly forsaken by God even while remembering that the fathers before him were never disappointed when they trusted God. He describes being mocked, surrounded like prey by wild animals, his strength failing, his body wasting away. In striking detail he describes his hands and feet being pierced and enemies casting lots for his clothing, images centuries before crucifixion existed as a Roman punishment. Then, without any narrated rescue, the psalm turns completely: David proclaims God's name, and the psalm expands outward until it pictures the whole world, even generations not yet born, turning to worship the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm should be read on two levels held together: it is David's own real experience of suffering and abandonment, expressed in the vivid imagery common to ancient Hebrew poetry, and it is also, by the Spirit's inspiration, genuine prophecy fulfilled with startling precision in the crucifixion of Jesus, who Himself quoted its opening line from the cross. The New Testament treats this psalm as messianic prophecy, not merely as a devotional parallel.
basic.originalLanguages: David's cry uses azab, forsaken, the very word Jesus quotes in Aramaic from the cross. The disputed but well attested reading karah, pierced, in verse 16 anticipates crucifixion centuries before it existed as a punishment, and the casting of goral, lots, for his clothing in verse 18 is fulfilled precisely by the Roman soldiers at the cross.
basic.typology: David's specific suffering, mockery, physical agony, pierced extremities, and divided garments, functions as a detailed prophetic pattern, a type, fulfilled point by point in the crucifixion of Christ, the true sufferer this psalm ultimately describes.
basic.christ: Matthew 27:46 records Jesus quoting this psalm's opening cry word for word from the cross. John 19:23-24 records Roman soldiers casting lots for His garment, fulfilling verse 18. Hebrews 2:12 quotes verse 22, 'I will proclaim Your name to my brothers,' applying it directly to Christ identifying with His people. The psalm's movement from abandonment to worldwide praise mirrors the movement from the cross to the great commission of Matthew 28.
basic.god: God appears here as both hidden, seemingly absent from the sufferer's cry, and as the one who has, in fact, not despised or hidden His face from the afflicted, verse 24, a tension resolved in the cross, where God the Son bears forsakenness so that forsaken sinners might be brought near.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 27:46 records Jesus quoting this psalm's opening words from the cross. John 19:23-24 fulfills the casting of lots for His garment. Hebrews 2:12 quotes verse 22 directly of Christ's identification with His brothers.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's descent into total forsakenness and its ascent into worldwide worship trace the whole gospel story in miniature: Christ bears the full weight of abandonment on the cross so that, through His resurrection, praise for God's righteousness might reach the ends of the earth and generations yet unborn.

context.literary: An individual lament of unusual structure, its first half, verses 1-21, moving through escalating distress and vivid physical description before an abrupt, unexplained turn at verse 22 into an expansive hymn of worldwide praise, verses 22-31, a shift some scholars believe reflects liturgical use where a priestly oracle of assurance was spoken between the two halves.
context.historical: Attributed to David, describing suffering so specific in its physical detail, pierced hands and feet, divided garments by lot, that it exceeds any recorded episode of David's own life, pointing beyond his personal experience to a fuller, future fulfillment.
context.canonical: The most quoted and alluded to psalm in the New Testament's passion narratives, cited or echoed in all four Gospels' crucifixion accounts and treated as direct messianic prophecy by the earliest church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, from David's own genuine experience of suffering and abandonment, a cry so deep and so specifically detailed that it functions, by the Spirit's inspiration, as prophecy of the ultimate righteous sufferer's crucifixion and vindication.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes both David's personal anguish and, more fully, the specific sufferings of Christ; its enduring, prescriptive truth is that God does not, in the end, hide His face from the afflicted, and that suffering borne in trust gives way to vindication and worldwide praise.

debates.question: Did David himself experience literal piercing of hands and feet, or does verse 16 describe suffering beyond his own personal history?

views.label: David describes his suffering in the vivid, exaggerated imagery typical of Hebrew lament poetry, being surrounded like prey by dogs and lions, without necessarily experiencing literal piercing himself, and the psalm's fullest, most literal fulfillment comes only in Christ's crucifixion
views.case: No episode in David's recorded life describes actual piercing of his hands and feet or enemies casting lots for his garments; the language functions poetically to describe severe distress and vulnerability, while its precise, literal fulfillment awaited the crucifixion of Jesus.

views.label: David may have experienced a genuine, severe physical assault approaching this description, making the psalm doubly rooted, real suffering in David's own life that also, by the Spirit's inspiration, prophetically anticipates Christ's crucifixion in even fuller and more literal detail
views.case: Some historic interpreters hold that David faced real physical danger and violence serious enough to inspire this vivid, specific language, without requiring that every detail be understood as pure poetic exaggeration, while still affirming its fuller and more precise fulfillment in Christ.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm, consistent with the New Testament's own extensive use of this psalm, that its fullest and most precise fulfillment is the crucifixion of Jesus Christ; the question concerns only how literally David's own experience already anticipated these specific details before their complete fulfillment in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: David's cry of azab, forsaken, becomes the very word Jesus quotes from the cross in Aramaic. The debated but ancient reading karah, pierced, in verse 16 anticipates crucifixion centuries before Rome adopted it as an execution method, and goral, lots, cast for his garments in verse 18, is fulfilled with striking precision by the soldiers at Golgotha.

typology.fulfillment: Christ's cry from the cross, quoting this exact verse, Matthew 27:46, as He bore the Father's judgment against sin in our place.

typology.type: Pierced hands and feet and garments divided by lot, verses 16-18
typology.fulfillment: The precise details of the crucifixion, John 19:23-24, fulfilled centuries after this psalm was written and before crucifixion existed as a punishment.

typology.type: The sufferer's proclamation of God's name to his brothers, verse 22
typology.fulfillment: Quoted directly of Christ in Hebrews 2:12, describing His identification with and proclamation of the Father's name to His redeemed brothers and sisters.

study.christ: This psalm is quoted more extensively in the New Testament's passion narratives than perhaps any other Old Testament text. Jesus Himself cries out its opening words from the cross, Matthew 27:46, entering fully into the experience of divine forsakenness so that those who trust Him need never bear it themselves. Its precise details, pierced hands and feet, garments divided by lot, mockery matching its exact wording, 'He trusts in the LORD, let Him deliver him,' Matthew 27:43, are fulfilled with startling accuracy at Golgotha. And its unexplained turn from abandonment to worldwide praise mirrors exactly the movement from the cross to the resurrection and the gospel's spread to the ends of the earth, Hebrews 2:12.
study.god: God appears here in profound tension: seemingly absent and unanswering to the sufferer's cries, yet ultimately declared not to have despised or hidden His face from the afflicted, verse 24. The cross resolves this tension completely: God the Son experiences real forsakenness so that forsaken sinners can be brought near to the Father forever.

crossReferences.note: Jesus quotes this psalm's opening cry word for word from the cross, in Aramaic, Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani.
Roman soldiers cast lots for Jesus's garment, fulfilling verse 18 with precise historical detail.
Quotes verse 22 directly, applying it to Christ's proclamation of the Father's name to His brothers, the redeemed.

study.oneStory: This psalm's descent into total forsakenness, described with startling precision that anticipates crucifixion centuries before it existed, and its unexplained ascent into worldwide praise, together trace the entire gospel story: Christ bears the full weight of abandonment on the cross so that His resurrection might bring praise for God's righteousness to the ends of the earth and to a people yet unborn.

sermon.title: Make Me Understand It!
sermon.why: Begg considers this psalm in light of its ultimate fulfillment in the Lord Jesus, who was rejected by men and bore the wrath of God in the place of sinners, showing that only through trusting in Christ's substitutionary death can sinners enjoy a restored relationship with God.

quiz.q: Where does the New Testament directly quote this psalm's opening cry?

options.text: Jesus quotes it word for word from the cross in Matthew 27:46, 'My God, my God, why have You forsaken Me?'
options.note: This is one of the most direct and significant quotations of an Old Testament psalm in all four Gospels' passion narratives.

options.text: Paul quotes it in his letter to the Romans regarding justification.
options.note: The direct quotation of this exact cry appears in Matthew's crucifixion account, not in Romans.

options.text: It is quoted by John the Baptist at Jesus's baptism.
options.note: This psalm's cry is quoted at the crucifixion, not at the baptism narrative.

options.text: It is never directly quoted in the New Testament.
options.note: This is incorrect; it is quoted directly and prominently in Matthew 27:46.

quiz.q: What specific detail in verse 16 anticipates crucifixion centuries before it existed as a punishment?

options.text: The piercing of hands and feet.
options.note: This detail, confirmed by ancient translations and fulfilled at the cross, is remarkable given crucifixion was not yet practiced when the psalm was written.

options.text: The mention of a crown of thorns.
options.note: The crown of thorns is a separate detail from the Gospel accounts, not found in this specific psalm.

options.text: A description of being buried in a borrowed tomb.
options.note: The burial detail comes from Isaiah 53, not from this specific verse in Psalm 22.

options.text: The exact number of lashes given before crucifixion.
options.note: This psalm does not specify a number of lashes; its precise, striking detail is the piercing of hands and feet.

quiz.q: What happens with the sufferer's garments in verse 18, and how is it fulfilled?

options.text: His garments are divided and lots are cast for his clothing, fulfilled precisely when Roman soldiers cast lots for Jesus's garment, John 19:23-24.
options.note: This is one of the most precise and historically documented fulfillments of Old Testament prophecy in the New Testament.

options.text: His garments are given away willingly as gifts to his followers.
options.note: The psalm describes enemies dividing and casting lots for garments, not the sufferer willingly giving gifts.

options.text: His garments are burned in judgment.
options.note: The text describes division and casting lots, not burning.

options.text: The psalm makes no mention of the sufferer's garments.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly addresses the division of garments and casting of lots.

quiz.q: How does the psalm's mood shift between verses 21 and 22?

options.text: It moves, without any narrated resolution, from desperate cries for rescue to confident proclamation of God's name and praise.
options.note: This abrupt but complete turn is one of the most striking features of the psalm's structure.

options.text: It moves from praise back into deeper despair with no resolution at all.
options.note: This reverses the actual direction of the psalm, which moves from despair toward praise, not the other way around.

options.text: The mood remains identical throughout the entire psalm with no shift.
options.note: The shift between the first and second halves of the psalm is one of its most notable and widely discussed features.

options.text: It shifts from praise to a detailed description of a specific military battle.
options.note: The shift is from lament to worship and worldwide praise, not to a military battle narrative.

quiz.q: What is the scope of the praise described in the psalm's closing verses, 27-31?

options.text: It reaches to all the ends of the earth, all the families of the nations, and a people yet unborn.
options.note: This expansive, worldwide, and even future generational scope mirrors the global reach of the gospel after Christ's resurrection.

options.text: It is limited strictly to the immediate members of David's own household.
options.note: The text explicitly expands to all the ends of the earth and generations yet unborn, far beyond David's household.

options.text: It applies only to the priestly tribe of Levi.
options.note: The scope described is universal, all nations and unborn generations, not limited to a single tribe.

options.text: It is restricted to a single future military victory.
options.note: The praise described is worship and proclamation of God's righteousness, not a military victory account.

questions: Jesus quoted this psalm's opening cry from the cross, entering fully into the experience of feeling forsaken. Does knowing that Christ Himself voiced this exact cry change how you bring your own feelings of God's absence to Him honestly?

The sufferer remembers that the fathers before him trusted and were never disappointed, even while his own present experience feels like abandonment. How do you hold together remembered faithfulness and present distress when God feels far away?
The mockers in this psalm taunt, 'He trusts in the LORD, let the LORD deliver him', words nearly repeated at the cross. Have you ever faced mockery for your faith, and how did you respond?

This psalm moves, without any narrated explanation, from utter despair to worldwide praise. Where in your own life have you experienced or hoped for that kind of unexplained turn from despair toward praise?

The psalm's closing vision reaches to a people yet unborn proclaiming God's righteousness. How does being part of that same ongoing story, generations still coming to praise God because of Christ, shape the way you think about your own part in passing on faith to others?

translationNotes.note: Pierced renders a Hebrew reading, karu, attested by the Septuagint and other ancient witnesses, differing by a single letter from an alternative reading sometimes rendered 'like a lion'; the piercing reading is strongly supported and finds a striking fulfillment in the crucifixion.

The casting of lots for the sufferer's garment is fulfilled precisely and historically by the Roman soldiers at the crucifixion, John 19:23-24, one of the New Testament's clearest direct fulfillments of psalm prophecy.
The source BSB text used an em dash before 'even those unable to preserve their lives'; here it is rendered with a comma to preserve the sense without using a dash.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 22:1-31. The source text contained one em dash in verse 29, converted to a comma per house style; no em or en dashes remain in this file. Sermon link verified this session via web_fetch of truthforlife.org.
